title: The Four Principles — POUR
learningObjectives: Define Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, and Robust in the context of course materials
Give a faculty-relevant example of a violation for each principle
Explain why these four categories cover virtually all accessibility issues

keyTerms.definition: The four foundational principles of WCAG. Every accessibility success criterion falls into one of these categories. Understanding POUR helps you think about accessibility without memorizing technical details.

A specific, testable accessibility requirement. WCAG 2.2 includes 86 success criteria organized under the POUR principles and grouped into guidelines. Each criterion has a conformance level: A, AA, or AAA.

A documented method that, if you follow it, will satisfy a success criterion. WCAG includes optional techniques for each criterion — they're guidance, not mandates. You may satisfy the criterion in other ways.

WCAG divides success criteria into three levels. Level A is the minimum; Level AA (required by Title II) includes A and AA criteria; Level AAA is the highest standard. Higher levels are more strict but sometimes apply only to specific content types.

In WCAG language, 'normative' means required (you must follow it), and 'informative' means advisory (you should follow it if possible). Normative parts include the success criteria themselves; informative parts include examples and techniques.

contentBlocks.title: The Four Questions Every Material Must Answer
contentBlocks.body:
WCAG organizes accessibility around four fundamental questions. If you can answer "yes" to all four, your material is likely accessible. If you answer "no" to any, you've found an accessibility barrier.

**Perceivable:** Can every student perceive the content? This means the content must be available to all senses. A video with sound is not perceivable to deaf students unless there are captions. An image with no description is not perceivable to blind students using a screen reader. A chart that only uses color (red for "required," green for "optional") is not perceivable to colorblind students. The question isn't "Is it visible?" — it's "Can it be perceived by everyone, including people with sensory disabilities?"

**Operable:** Can every student navigate and interact with the material? This means you can use a keyboard instead of a mouse, you can navigate at your own speed, and you're not trapped by auto-playing timers or elements that can't be reached by tabbing. A quiz with drag-and-drop questions is not operable by keyboard users. A 30-second auto-advancing slideshow is not operable for someone who reads slowly. An online form where the focus indicator is invisible is not operable because you can't see where you are.

**Understandable:** Can every student comprehend the material? This is about both language and function. If your syllabus is written in jargon without explanation, it's not understandable. If a form doesn't tell you what went wrong when you submit it incorrectly, it's not understandable. If the reading level is too high for your audience, it's not understandable — not because it's wrong, but because the barrier exists.

**Robust:** Does your material work with assistive technology? A screen reader is software that speaks the content of your document aloud. If your PDF was made from a scanned image with no OCR, a screen reader can't read it — the PDF isn't robust. A PowerPoint where images have no alt text is not robust because someone using a screen reader won't know what the image contains. An interactive online tool that only works in Firefox, not with assistive technology, is not robust.

contentBlocks.title: The Four POUR Principles
contentBlocks.imageAlt: Two-by-two grid of the four WCAG principles — Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, and Robust — each paired with a common classroom example of a failure. The first letters form the acronym POUR.
contentBlocks.imageCaption: Every WCAG success criterion answers one of four POUR questions.

contentBlocks.title: POUR in a Typical Syllabus
contentBlocks.body:
Let's walk through a real syllabus and see one violation per POUR principle. This is not a bad syllabus — it's a typical one.

**Perceivable violation:** The syllabus includes a logo image — the department seal or your university's name in a graphical format. The image has no alt text. A screen reader user cannot perceive what the image is. The fix: Add a brief alt text describing the image (for a logo, "[Institution Name] Department of [Subject]" is usually sufficient).

**Operable violation:** The "Course Schedule" section is a table with clickable links. You built it beautifully — but you placed those links inside table cells with no way to tab directly to them. A keyboard user can tab to the table, but then has to navigate into each cell individually. It's not impossible, but it's inefficient and frustrating. The fix: Make sure links inside tables are reachable by tabbing normally, or use a different layout for the schedule.

**Understandable violation:** Your attendance policy says, "Students must maintain satisfactory attendance in accordance with the University attendance standard policy linked above." The link goes to a 12-page PDF full of jargon and exceptions. A first-year student reads this and has no idea what "satisfactory" means. Do you lose points after 3 absences or 5? The fix: Spell it out plainly in your syllabus: "Attendance is required. More than 3 unexcused absences will lower your grade by one letter. Excused absences (illness, family emergency, university-approved events) do not count."

**Robust violation:** You saved your syllabus as a PDF by exporting from Word. The original Word document had proper heading structure, but the PDF export scrambled the reading order. A screen reader reads the content in a strange, non-linear order that makes no sense. The fix: Either keep the syllabus in Word format (not exported to PDF), or export to PDF carefully, checking the reading order afterward.

contentBlocks.title: How WCAG Is Organized
contentBlocks.body:
WCAG has a structured hierarchy that can seem complicated at first, but once you understand it, you'll find it's actually useful. Here's how it works:

**Principles** are the broadest level — the four POUR principles. Each principle covers a basic category of accessibility.

**Guidelines** sit under each principle. There are 13 guidelines total in WCAG 2.2. For example, under Perceivable, one guideline is "Guideline 1.4: Distinguishable," which covers topics like color contrast and text spacing.

**Success Criteria** sit under each guideline and are the actual testable requirements. This is where the technical work happens. For example, under Guideline 1.4, one success criterion is "1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)," which requires a specific ratio between text color and background color.

**Techniques** are below success criteria and are optional examples of how to satisfy the criterion. WCAG publishes techniques as helpful guidance, but they're not required — you can satisfy a criterion in other ways.

You don't need to memorize this structure. But it helps to know that when you see a criterion number like "1.4.3," the number tells you something:

- The **first digit** (1) tells you the principle: 1 = Perceivable, 2 = Operable, 3 = Understandable, 4 = Robust.
- The **second digit** (4) tells you the guideline number under that principle.
- The **last digits** (3) tell you which criterion under that guideline.

So "1.4.3" means "Perceivable, Guideline 4, Criterion 3" — which is "Contrast (Minimum)." Once you see the pattern a few times, you can guess the principle just from the number. This is useful when someone says, "Your document violates 2.4.7" — you immediately know it's an Operable issue (Principle 2), probably about navigation (Guideline 4).

POUR — Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust — is the foundation of WCAG. Every accessibility success criterion fits into one of these four categories. Understanding POUR helps you think like an accessibility specialist without memorizing technical details. When you encounter a WCAG criterion number, the first digit tells you the principle, which helps you understand what kind of accessibility issue you're addressing.

freeTextPrompt.prompt: Pick one of the four POUR principles and find an example of a violation in your own course materials, your institution's website, or a website you use regularly. Describe what the violation is and which principle it falls under. Why do you think this violation happened — was it a knowledge gap, a tool limitation, or something else?
freeTextPrompt.rubric: A strong response identifies a specific, real violation (not hypothetical) and correctly maps it to one POUR principle. It describes the violation concretely (not vaguely) and reflects thoughtfully on why it might have occurred. Bonus: the response shows understanding that the violation creates a barrier for a specific group of users.
